title: Why is the LLM cost model fundamentally different from traditional ML hosting, and how do you control it?
prompt:
Why is the LLM cost model fundamentally different from traditional ML hosting, and how do you control it?

*Answer in the editor as you would out loud in an interview (a few sentences to a short paragraph is fine). Then use 💡 Hint, 🧠 AI feedback, or 📗 Show answer.*
reference_solution:
Traditional ML server hosting has mostly fixed compute costs. LLM APIs scale directly with token usage, creating variable and highly asymmetric costs. What drives cost: variable output length (a 2k-word response costs 10x more than a 200-word one); system prompts charged on every single request; chat history that grows linearly with turns; RAG chunks that each inject N tokens per query; and model tiering, where GPT-4-class models can be 10-20x pricier than cheaper tiers for the same request. How you control it: context caps (hard limits on history, chunks, and max output tokens), model routing (simple queries to cheap models, complex ones to premium), caching (serve cached replies for identical/similar queries), prompt compression (keep system prompts lean since every sentence costs money on every call), and feature attribution (monitor cost per feature to find where spend concentrates).

**Takeaway:** LLM cost is a per-request, per-token variable cost, not a fixed hosting cost — so cost control lives in prompt design and request routing, not just infrastructure sizing.
hint_notes: Cost scales with tokens (output length, system prompt, history, RAG chunks, model tier) — control via context caps, model routing, caching, prompt compression, and cost-per-feature attribution.
